Alternatively, alight at [[:en:Ozuki Station|Kōzuki Station]] or [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]] on the [[:en:San'yō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11386420]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2016-03-25}} {{infobox Onsen |Name = Tawarayama Onsen |image = Tawarayama-spa.jpg |Comment = Tawarayama Onsen Town (in front of the {{ill|Public Bath|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} "Machi no Yu") |Location = [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nagato, Yamaguchi|Nagato City]] (formerly [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]) {{Location map many | Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture | relief = 1 | caption = | width = 220 | float = center | lat_deg = 34.290833 | lon_deg = 131.100208 | pos = bottom | label = Tawarayama Onsen (俵山温泉) | lat2_deg = 34.172801 | lon2_deg = 131.480986 | mark2 = blue pog.svg | pos2 = right | label2 = [[:en:Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|Yamaguchi Station]] }}
Map of Yamaguchi Prefecture
|Latitude Degrees = 34 |Latitude Minutes = 17 |Latitude Seconds = 27 |Longitude Degrees = 131 |Longitude Minutes = 6 |Longitude Seconds = 0.8 |Access = 　• Air: [[:en:Yamaguchi Ube Airport|Yamaguchi Ube Airport]]
　• Railway: [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] – from [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]]
　• Bus: {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} – Bus stop "Tawarayama Onsen"
　• Automobile: [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] – from {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|lt=Mine IC|lt_ja=美祢IC|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} |Water Type = {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} |Temperature = 41 |Discharge = |pH = 9.9 |Liquid Classification = Alkaline |Osmotic Pressure Classification = Low Tonicity |Number of Accommodations = |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = |Statistical Year = |External links = [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ http://tawarayamaonsen.com/] |Special Notes = }} '''Tawarayama Onsen''' (たわらやまおんせん) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagato, Yamaguchi|Nagato City]] (formerly [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]). It is one of the "Bōchō Four Hot Springs" ({{ill|Bōchō Four Hot Springs|ja|防長四湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōchō Four Hot Springs|1=Bōchō Four Hot Springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65291872}}) representing Yamaguchi Prefecture (which also includes the former Suō Province). Its water quality is tailored for {{ill|Therapeutic Bathing|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic Bathing|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}, being reputed for its efficacy in relieving [[:en:Neuralgia|Neuralgia]] and [[:en:Rheumatism|Rheumatism]]. In the 1886 Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles (『日本鉱泉誌』), it was introduced as "Yumachi Kōsen" (湯町鉱泉). == Water Quality == {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}. The two principal sources—"Machi no Yu-sen" and "Kawa no Yu-sen"—both exhibit a high alkalinity with a pH of 9.9 and a temperature of 41 [[:en:Celsius|Celsius]], making them among the few high-temperature sources in Yamaguchi Prefecture. According to the 1922 Nagato Railway Guide (『長門鉄道案内』), the two sources differ in their therapeutic effects. Note that the water is not pumped but is self-emerging. == Onsen Town == A ryokan district is set in a quiet mountainous area along the upper reaches of the {{ill|Kiyagawa|ja|木屋川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyagawa|1=Koya River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518298}}. As of 2022, there are 16 ryokan, reduced to less than half of the approximately 40 present 30 years ago. Unlike {{ill|Nagato Yumoto Onsen|ja|長門湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Yumoto Onsen|1=Nagato Yumoto Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654891}}, where large hotels dominate, most accommodations here are small Japanese-style ryokan catering to individuals. {{ill|Tadatoshi Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoshi Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}} remarked, "I know of no other hot spring town where the original landscape of hot springs for the Japanese has been so magnificently preserved as in Tawarayama,"『温泉教授・松田忠徳の古湯を歩く』 p.184 and he highly praises this town—along with {{ill|Hijiori Onsen|ja|肘折温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiori Onsen|1=Hijiori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611454}} (in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]])—for maintaining its character and function as a therapeutic bathing site. == History == According to the hot spring founding legend recounted in the 1904 "Tawarayama Onsen-shi," during the Ōei era of the Muromachi period, a fisherman once shot an arrow at a white monkey which escaped. Later, the wounded white monkey was seen straddling a rock and repeatedly pouring clear spring water onto its arrow wound. As the fisherman approached to capture the animal, the white monkey emitted a radiant light, transforming into a Buddhist figure and departing on a purple cloud. Astonished, the fisherman grasped the water from the spring—only to find it warm and evaporating. He then rushed to the nearby {{ill|Nōman-ji|ja|能満寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōman-ji|1=Nōman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611983}} and related the event. The temple abbot explained, "By the vow of our temple’s founding priest, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], our principal image, [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]], has manifested in the form of a white monkey to reveal a sacred spring effective against all diseases, and to instruct those engaged in killing in the principles of compassion. If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] via the [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] from either [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]] or, alternatively, via the [[:en:San'in Main Line|Sanin Main Line]]/Mine Line from [[:en:Nagatoshi Station|Nagato City Station]]. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "[[:en:Ozuki Station|Kozuki Station]]," then a limited express to [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]]; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at [[:en:Ozuki Station|Kōzuki Station]] or [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]] on the [[:en:San'yō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] via the [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] from either [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]] or, alternatively, via the [[:en:San'in Main Line|Sanin Main Line]]/Mine Line from [[:en:Nagatoshi Station|Nagato City Station]]. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "[[:en:Ozuki Station|Kozuki Station]]," then a limited express to [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]]; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at [[:en:Ozuki Station|Kōzuki Station]] or [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]] on the [[:en:San'yō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11386420]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2016-03-25}} {{infobox Onsen |Name = Tawarayama Onsen |image = Tawarayama-spa.jpg |Comment = Tawarayama Onsen Town (in front of the {{ill|Public Bath|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} "Machi no Yu") |Location = [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nagato, Yamaguchi|Nagato City]] (formerly [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]) {{Location map many | Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture | relief = 1 | caption = | width = 220 | float = center | lat_deg = 34.290833 | lon_deg = 131.100208 | pos = bottom | label = Tawarayama Onsen (俵山温泉) | lat2_deg = 34.172801 | lon2_deg = 131.480986 | mark2 = blue pog.svg | pos2 = right | label2 = [[:en:Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|Yamaguchi Station]] }}
Map of Yamaguchi Prefecture
|Latitude Degrees = 34 |Latitude Minutes = 17 |Latitude Seconds = 27 |Longitude Degrees = 131 |Longitude Minutes = 6 |Longitude Seconds = 0.8 |Access = 　• Air: [[:en:Yamaguchi Ube Airport|Yamaguchi Ube Airport]]
　• Railway: [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] – from [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]]
　• Bus: {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} – Bus stop "Tawarayama Onsen"
　• Automobile: [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] – from {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|lt=Mine IC|lt_ja=美祢IC|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} |Water Type = {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} |Temperature = 41 |Discharge = |pH = 9.9 |Liquid Classification = Alkaline |Osmotic Pressure Classification = Low Tonicity |Number of Accommodations = |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = |Statistical Year = |External links = [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ http://tawarayamaonsen.com/] |Special Notes = }} '''Tawarayama Onsen''' (たわらやまおんせん) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagato, Yamaguchi|Nagato City]] (formerly [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]). It is one of the "Bōchō Four Hot Springs" ({{ill|Bōchō Four Hot Springs|ja|防長四湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōchō Four Hot Springs|1=Bōchō Four Hot Springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65291872}}) representing Yamaguchi Prefecture (which also includes the former Suō Province). Its water quality is tailored for {{ill|Therapeutic Bathing|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic Bathing|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}, being reputed for its efficacy in relieving [[:en:Neuralgia|Neuralgia]] and [[:en:Rheumatism|Rheumatism]]. In the 1886 Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles (『日本鉱泉誌』), it was introduced as "Yumachi Kōsen" (湯町鉱泉). == Water Quality == {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}. The two principal sources—"Machi no Yu-sen" and "Kawa no Yu-sen"—both exhibit a high alkalinity with a pH of 9.9 and a temperature of 41 [[:en:Celsius|Celsius]], making them among the few high-temperature sources in Yamaguchi Prefecture. According to the 1922 Nagato Railway Guide (『長門鉄道案内』), the two sources differ in their therapeutic effects. Note that the water is not pumped but is self-emerging. == Onsen Town == A ryokan district is set in a quiet mountainous area along the upper reaches of the {{ill|Kiyagawa|ja|木屋川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyagawa|1=Koya River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518298}}. As of 2022, there are 16 ryokan, reduced to less than half of the approximately 40 present 30 years ago. Unlike {{ill|Nagato Yumoto Onsen|ja|長門湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Yumoto Onsen|1=Nagato Yumoto Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654891}}, where large hotels dominate, most accommodations here are small Japanese-style ryokan catering to individuals. {{ill|Tadatoshi Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoshi Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}} remarked, "I know of no other hot spring town where the original landscape of hot springs for the Japanese has been so magnificently preserved as in Tawarayama,"『温泉教授・松田忠徳の古湯を歩く』 p.184 and he highly praises this town—along with {{ill|Hijiori Onsen|ja|肘折温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiori Onsen|1=Hijiori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611454}} (in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]])—for maintaining its character and function as a therapeutic bathing site. == History == According to the hot spring founding legend recounted in the 1904 "Tawarayama Onsen-shi," during the Ōei era of the Muromachi period, a fisherman once shot an arrow at a white monkey which escaped. Later, the wounded white monkey was seen straddling a rock and repeatedly pouring clear spring water onto its arrow wound. As the fisherman approached to capture the animal, the white monkey emitted a radiant light, transforming into a Buddhist figure and departing on a purple cloud. Astonished, the fisherman grasped the water from the spring—only to find it warm and evaporating. He then rushed to the nearby {{ill|Nōman-ji|ja|能満寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōman-ji|1=Nōman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611983}} and related the event. The temple abbot explained, "By the vow of our temple’s founding priest, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], our principal image, [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]], has manifested in the form of a white monkey to reveal a sacred spring effective against all diseases, and to instruct those engaged in killing in the principles of compassion. If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] via the [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] from either [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]] or, alternatively, via the [[:en:San'in Main Line|Sanin Main Line]]/Mine Line from [[:en:Nagatoshi Station|Nagato City Station]]. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "[[:en:Ozuki Station|Kozuki Station]]," then a limited express to [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]]; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at [[:en:Ozuki Station|Kōzuki Station]] or [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]] on the [[:en:San'yō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] via the [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] from either [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]] or, alternatively, via the [[:en:San'in Main Line|Sanin Main Line]]/Mine Line from [[:en:Nagatoshi Station|Nagato City Station]]. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "[[:en:Ozuki Station|Kozuki Station]]," then a limited express to [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]]; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at [[:en:Ozuki Station|Kōzuki Station]] or [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]] on the [[:en:San'yō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11386420]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2016-03-25}} {{infobox Onsen |Name = Tawarayama Onsen |image = Tawarayama-spa.jpg |Comment = Tawarayama Onsen Town (in front of the {{ill|Public Bath|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} "Machi no Yu") |Location = [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nagato, Yamaguchi|Nagato City]] (formerly [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]) {{Location map many | Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture | relief = 1 | caption = | width = 220 | float = center | lat_deg = 34.290833 | lon_deg = 131.100208 | pos = bottom | label = Tawarayama Onsen (俵山温泉) | lat2_deg = 34.172801 | lon2_deg = 131.480986 | mark2 = blue pog.svg | pos2 = right | label2 = [[:en:Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|Yamaguchi Station]] }}
Map of Yamaguchi Prefecture
|Latitude Degrees = 34 |Latitude Minutes = 17 |Latitude Seconds = 27 |Longitude Degrees = 131 |Longitude Minutes = 6 |Longitude Seconds = 0.8 |Access = 　• Air: [[:en:Yamaguchi Ube Airport|Yamaguchi Ube Airport]]
　• Railway: [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] – from [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]]
　• Bus: {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} – Bus stop "Tawarayama Onsen"
　• Automobile: [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] – from {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|lt=Mine IC|lt_ja=美祢IC|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} |Water Type = {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} |Temperature = 41 |Discharge = |pH = 9.9 |Liquid Classification = Alkaline |Osmotic Pressure Classification = Low Tonicity |Number of Accommodations = |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = |Statistical Year = |External links = [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ http://tawarayamaonsen.com/] |Special Notes = }} '''Tawarayama Onsen''' (たわらやまおんせん) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagato, Yamaguchi|Nagato City]] (formerly [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]). It is one of the "Bōchō Four Hot Springs" ({{ill|Bōchō Four Hot Springs|ja|防長四湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōchō Four Hot Springs|1=Bōchō Four Hot Springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65291872}}) representing Yamaguchi Prefecture (which also includes the former Suō Province). Its water quality is tailored for {{ill|Therapeutic Bathing|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic Bathing|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}, being reputed for its efficacy in relieving [[:en:Neuralgia|Neuralgia]] and [[:en:Rheumatism|Rheumatism]]. In the 1886 Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles (『日本鉱泉誌』), it was introduced as "Yumachi Kōsen" (湯町鉱泉). == Water Quality == {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}. The two principal sources—"Machi no Yu-sen" and "Kawa no Yu-sen"—both exhibit a high alkalinity with a pH of 9.9 and a temperature of 41 [[:en:Celsius|Celsius]], making them among the few high-temperature sources in Yamaguchi Prefecture. According to the 1922 Nagato Railway Guide (『長門鉄道案内』), the two sources differ in their therapeutic effects. Note that the water is not pumped but is self-emerging. == Onsen Town == A ryokan district is set in a quiet mountainous area along the upper reaches of the {{ill|Kiyagawa|ja|木屋川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyagawa|1=Koya River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518298}}. As of 2022, there are 16 ryokan, reduced to less than half of the approximately 40 present 30 years ago. Unlike {{ill|Nagato Yumoto Onsen|ja|長門湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Yumoto Onsen|1=Nagato Yumoto Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654891}}, where large hotels dominate, most accommodations here are small Japanese-style ryokan catering to individuals. {{ill|Tadatoshi Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoshi Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}} remarked, "I know of no other hot spring town where the original landscape of hot springs for the Japanese has been so magnificently preserved as in Tawarayama,"『温泉教授・松田忠徳の古湯を歩く』 p.184 and he highly praises this town—along with {{ill|Hijiori Onsen|ja|肘折温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiori Onsen|1=Hijiori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611454}} (in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]])—for maintaining its character and function as a therapeutic bathing site. == History == According to the hot spring founding legend recounted in the 1904 "Tawarayama Onsen-shi," during the Ōei era of the Muromachi period, a fisherman once shot an arrow at a white monkey which escaped. Later, the wounded white monkey was seen straddling a rock and repeatedly pouring clear spring water onto its arrow wound. As the fisherman approached to capture the animal, the white monkey emitted a radiant light, transforming into a Buddhist figure and departing on a purple cloud. Astonished, the fisherman grasped the water from the spring—only to find it warm and evaporating. He then rushed to the nearby {{ill|Nōman-ji|ja|能満寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōman-ji|1=Nōman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611983}} and related the event. The temple abbot explained, "By the vow of our temple’s founding priest, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], our principal image, [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]], has manifested in the form of a white monkey to reveal a sacred spring effective against all diseases, and to instruct those engaged in killing in the principles of compassion. If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] via the [[:en:Mine Line|Mine Line]] from either [[:en:Nagato-Yumoto Station|Nagato Yumoto Station]] or, alternatively, via the [[:en:San'in Main Line|Sanin Main Line]]/Mine Line from [[:en:Nagatoshi Station|Nagato City Station]]. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "[[:en:Ozuki Station|Kozuki Station]]," then a limited express to [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]]; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at [[:en:Ozuki Station|Kōzuki Station]] or [[:en:Shimonoseki Station|Shimonoseki Station]] on the [[:en:San'yō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the [[:en:Chūgoku Expressway|Chūgoku Expressway]] via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

Unlike {{ill|Nagato Yumoto Onsen|ja|長門湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Yumoto Onsen|1=Nagato Yumoto Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654891}}, where large hotels dominate, most accommodations here are small Japanese-style ryokan catering to individuals. {{ill|Tadatoshi Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoshi Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}} remarked, "I know of no other hot spring town where the original landscape of hot springs for the Japanese has been so magnificently preserved as in Tawarayama,"『温泉教授・松田忠徳の古湯を歩く』 p.184 and he highly praises this town—along with {{ill|Hijiori Onsen|ja|肘折温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiori Onsen|1=Hijiori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611454}} (in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}})—for maintaining its character and function as a therapeutic bathing site. == History == According to the hot spring founding legend recounted in the 1904 "Tawarayama Onsen-shi," during the Ōei era of the Muromachi period, a fisherman once shot an arrow at a white monkey which escaped. Later, the wounded white monkey was seen straddling a rock and repeatedly pouring clear spring water onto its arrow wound. As the fisherman approached to capture the animal, the white monkey emitted a radiant light, transforming into a Buddhist figure and departing on a purple cloud. Astonished, the fisherman grasped the water from the spring—only to find it warm and evaporating. He then rushed to the nearby {{ill|Nōman-ji|ja|能満寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōman-ji|1=Nōman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611983}} and related the event. The temple abbot explained, "By the vow of our temple’s founding priest, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, our principal image, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, has manifested in the form of a white monkey to reveal a sacred spring effective against all diseases, and to instruct those engaged in killing in the principles of compassion. If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}} via the {{ill|Mine Line|en|Mine Line|WD=Q898907|lt=Mine Line}} from either {{ill|Nagato-Yumoto Station|en|Nagato-Yumoto Station|WD=Q11654892|lt=Nagato Yumoto Station}} or, alternatively, via the {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=Sanin Main Line}}/Mine Line from {{ill|Nagatoshi Station|en|Nagatoshi Station|WD=Q6958700|lt=Nagato City Station}}. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "{{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kozuki Station}}," then a limited express to {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}}; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at {{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kōzuki Station}} or {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}} on the {{ill|San'yō Main Line|en|San'yō Main Line|WD=Q1193436|lt=Sanyō Main Line}} and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the {{ill|Chūgoku Expressway|en|Chūgoku Expressway|WD=Q1091207|lt=Chūgoku Expressway}} via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11386420]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2016-03-25}} {{infobox Onsen |Name = Tawarayama Onsen |image = Tawarayama-spa.jpg |Comment = Tawarayama Onsen Town (in front of the {{ill|Public Bath|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} "Machi no Yu") |Location = {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}} {{ill|Nagato, Yamaguchi|en|Nagato, Yamaguchi|WD=Q390812|lt=Nagato City}} (formerly {{ill|Nagato Province|en|Nagato Province|WD=Q907382|lt=Nagato Province}}) {{Location map many | Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture | relief = 1 | caption = | width = 220 | float = center | lat_deg = 34.290833 | lon_deg = 131.100208 | pos = bottom | label = Tawarayama Onsen (俵山温泉) | lat2_deg = 34.172801 | lon2_deg = 131.480986 | mark2 = blue pog.svg | pos2 = right | label2 = {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|en|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|WD=Q8192374|lt=Yamaguchi Station}} }}
Map of Yamaguchi Prefecture
|Latitude Degrees = 34 |Latitude Minutes = 17 |Latitude Seconds = 27 |Longitude Degrees = 131 |Longitude Minutes = 6 |Longitude Seconds = 0.8 |Access = 　• Air: {{ill|Yamaguchi Ube Airport|en|Yamaguchi Ube Airport|WD=Q1328906|lt=Yamaguchi Ube Airport}}
　• Railway: {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Mine Line|en|Mine Line|WD=Q898907|lt=Mine Line}} – from {{ill|Nagato-Yumoto Station|en|Nagato-Yumoto Station|WD=Q11654892|lt=Nagato Yumoto Station}}
　• Bus: {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} – Bus stop "Tawarayama Onsen"
　• Automobile: {{ill|Chūgoku Expressway|en|Chūgoku Expressway|WD=Q1091207|lt=Chūgoku Expressway}} – from {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|lt=Mine IC|lt_ja=美祢IC|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} |Water Type = {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} |Temperature = 41 |Discharge = |pH = 9.9 |Liquid Classification = Alkaline |Osmotic Pressure Classification = Low Tonicity |Number of Accommodations = |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = |Statistical Year = |External links = [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ http://tawarayamaonsen.com/] |Special Notes = }} '''Tawarayama Onsen''' (たわらやまおんせん) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagato, Yamaguchi|en|Nagato, Yamaguchi|WD=Q390812|lt=Nagato City}} (formerly {{ill|Nagato Province|en|Nagato Province|WD=Q907382|lt=Nagato Province}}). It is one of the "Bōchō Four Hot Springs" ({{ill|Bōchō Four Hot Springs|ja|防長四湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōchō Four Hot Springs|1=Bōchō Four Hot Springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65291872}}) representing Yamaguchi Prefecture (which also includes the former Suō Province). Its water quality is tailored for {{ill|Therapeutic Bathing|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic Bathing|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}, being reputed for its efficacy in relieving {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia|WD=Q1136940|lt=Neuralgia}} and {{ill|Rheumatism|en|Rheumatism|WD=Q684924|lt=Rheumatism}}. In the 1886 Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles (『日本鉱泉誌』), it was introduced as "Yumachi Kōsen" (湯町鉱泉). == Water Quality == {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}. The two principal sources—"Machi no Yu-sen" and "Kawa no Yu-sen"—both exhibit a high alkalinity with a pH of 9.9 and a temperature of 41 {{ill|Celsius|en|Celsius|WD=Q25267|lt=Celsius}}, making them among the few high-temperature sources in Yamaguchi Prefecture. According to the 1922 Nagato Railway Guide (『長門鉄道案内』), the two sources differ in their therapeutic effects. Note that the water is not pumped but is self-emerging. == Onsen Town == A ryokan district is set in a quiet mountainous area along the upper reaches of the {{ill|Kiyagawa|ja|木屋川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyagawa|1=Koya River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518298}}. As of 2022, there are 16 ryokan, reduced to less than half of the approximately 40 present 30 years ago. Unlike {{ill|Nagato Yumoto Onsen|ja|長門湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Yumoto Onsen|1=Nagato Yumoto Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654891}}, where large hotels dominate, most accommodations here are small Japanese-style ryokan catering to individuals. {{ill|Tadatoshi Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoshi Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}} remarked, "I know of no other hot spring town where the original landscape of hot springs for the Japanese has been so magnificently preserved as in Tawarayama,"『温泉教授・松田忠徳の古湯を歩く』 p.184 and he highly praises this town—along with {{ill|Hijiori Onsen|ja|肘折温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiori Onsen|1=Hijiori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611454}} (in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}})—for maintaining its character and function as a therapeutic bathing site. == History == According to the hot spring founding legend recounted in the 1904 "Tawarayama Onsen-shi," during the Ōei era of the Muromachi period, a fisherman once shot an arrow at a white monkey which escaped. Later, the wounded white monkey was seen straddling a rock and repeatedly pouring clear spring water onto its arrow wound. As the fisherman approached to capture the animal, the white monkey emitted a radiant light, transforming into a Buddhist figure and departing on a purple cloud. Astonished, the fisherman grasped the water from the spring—only to find it warm and evaporating. He then rushed to the nearby {{ill|Nōman-ji|ja|能満寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōman-ji|1=Nōman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611983}} and related the event. The temple abbot explained, "By the vow of our temple’s founding priest, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, our principal image, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, has manifested in the form of a white monkey to reveal a sacred spring effective against all diseases, and to instruct those engaged in killing in the principles of compassion. If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}} via the {{ill|Mine Line|en|Mine Line|WD=Q898907|lt=Mine Line}} from either {{ill|Nagato-Yumoto Station|en|Nagato-Yumoto Station|WD=Q11654892|lt=Nagato Yumoto Station}} or, alternatively, via the {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=Sanin Main Line}}/Mine Line from {{ill|Nagatoshi Station|en|Nagatoshi Station|WD=Q6958700|lt=Nagato City Station}}. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "{{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kozuki Station}}," then a limited express to {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}}; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at {{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kōzuki Station}} or {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}} on the {{ill|San'yō Main Line|en|San'yō Main Line|WD=Q1193436|lt=Sanyō Main Line}} and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the {{ill|Chūgoku Expressway|en|Chūgoku Expressway|WD=Q1091207|lt=Chūgoku Expressway}} via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[ja:俵山温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11386420]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2016-03-25}} {{infobox Onsen |Name = Tawarayama Onsen |image = Tawarayama-spa.jpg |Comment = Tawarayama Onsen Town (in front of the {{ill|Public Bath|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} "Machi no Yu") |Location = {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}} {{ill|Nagato, Yamaguchi|en|Nagato, Yamaguchi|WD=Q390812|lt=Nagato City}} (formerly {{ill|Nagato Province|en|Nagato Province|WD=Q907382|lt=Nagato Province}}) {{Location map many | Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture | relief = 1 | caption = | width = 220 | float = center | lat_deg = 34.290833 | lon_deg = 131.100208 | pos = bottom | label = Tawarayama Onsen (俵山温泉) | lat2_deg = 34.172801 | lon2_deg = 131.480986 | mark2 = blue pog.svg | pos2 = right | label2 = {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|en|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|WD=Q8192374|lt=Yamaguchi Station}} }}

|Latitude Degrees = 34 |Latitude Minutes = 17 |Latitude Seconds = 27 |Longitude Degrees = 131 |Longitude Minutes = 6 |Longitude Seconds = 0.8 |Access = 　• Air: {{ill|Yamaguchi Ube Airport|en|Yamaguchi Ube Airport|WD=Q1328906|lt=Yamaguchi Ube Airport}}
　• Railway: {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Mine Line|en|Mine Line|WD=Q898907|lt=Mine Line}} – from {{ill|Nagato-Yumoto Station|en|Nagato-Yumoto Station|WD=Q11654892|lt=Nagato Yumoto Station}}
　• Bus: {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} – Bus stop "Tawarayama Onsen"
　• Automobile: {{ill|Chūgoku Expressway|en|Chūgoku Expressway|WD=Q1091207|lt=Chūgoku Expressway}} – from {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|lt=Mine IC|lt_ja=美祢IC|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} |Water Type = {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} |Temperature = 41 |Discharge = |pH = 9.9 |Liquid Classification = Alkaline |Osmotic Pressure Classification = Low Tonicity |Number of Accommodations = |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = |Statistical Year = |External links = [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ http://tawarayamaonsen.com/] |Special Notes = }} '''Tawarayama Onsen''' (たわらやまおんせん) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagato, Yamaguchi|en|Nagato, Yamaguchi|WD=Q390812|lt=Nagato City}} (formerly {{ill|Nagato Province|en|Nagato Province|WD=Q907382|lt=Nagato Province}}). It is one of the "Bōchō Four Hot Springs" ({{ill|Bōchō Four Hot Springs|ja|防長四湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōchō Four Hot Springs|1=Bōchō Four Hot Springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65291872}}) representing Yamaguchi Prefecture (which also includes the former Suō Province). Its water quality is tailored for {{ill|Therapeutic Bathing|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic Bathing|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}, being reputed for its efficacy in relieving {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia|WD=Q1136940|lt=Neuralgia}} and {{ill|Rheumatism|en|Rheumatism|WD=Q684924|lt=Rheumatism}}. In the 1886 Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles (『日本鉱泉誌』), it was introduced as "Yumachi Kōsen" (湯町鉱泉). == Water Quality == {{ill|Alkaline|ja|アルカリ性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alkaline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩基|1=base|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11193}} {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}. The two principal sources—"Machi no Yu-sen" and "Kawa no Yu-sen"—both exhibit a high alkalinity with a pH of 9.9 and a temperature of 41 {{ill|Celsius|en|Celsius|WD=Q25267|lt=Celsius}}, making them among the few high-temperature sources in Yamaguchi Prefecture. According to the 1922 Nagato Railway Guide (『長門鉄道案内』), the two sources differ in their therapeutic effects. Note that the water is not pumped but is self-emerging. == Onsen Town == A ryokan district is set in a quiet mountainous area along the upper reaches of the {{ill|Kiyagawa|ja|木屋川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyagawa|1=Koya River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518298}}. As of 2022, there are 16 ryokan, reduced to less than half of the approximately 40 present 30 years ago. Unlike {{ill|Nagato Yumoto Onsen|ja|長門湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Yumoto Onsen|1=Nagato Yumoto Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654891}}, where large hotels dominate, most accommodations here are small Japanese-style ryokan catering to individuals. {{ill|Tadatoshi Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoshi Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}} remarked, "I know of no other hot spring town where the original landscape of hot springs for the Japanese has been so magnificently preserved as in Tawarayama,"『温泉教授・松田忠徳の古湯を歩く』 p.184 and he highly praises this town—along with {{ill|Hijiori Onsen|ja|肘折温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiori Onsen|1=Hijiori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611454}} (in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}})—for maintaining its character and function as a therapeutic bathing site. == History == According to the hot spring founding legend recounted in the 1904 "Tawarayama Onsen-shi," during the Ōei era of the Muromachi period, a fisherman once shot an arrow at a white monkey which escaped. Later, the wounded white monkey was seen straddling a rock and repeatedly pouring clear spring water onto its arrow wound. As the fisherman approached to capture the animal, the white monkey emitted a radiant light, transforming into a Buddhist figure and departing on a purple cloud. Astonished, the fisherman grasped the water from the spring—only to find it warm and evaporating. He then rushed to the nearby {{ill|Nōman-ji|ja|能満寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōman-ji|1=Nōman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611983}} and related the event. The temple abbot explained, "By the vow of our temple’s founding priest, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, our principal image, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, has manifested in the form of a white monkey to reveal a sacred spring effective against all diseases, and to instruct those engaged in killing in the principles of compassion. If you cease your life as a fisherman and cultivate the mountains to aid the ailing, your sins will be expiated." Moved, the fisherman gathered the villagers, cleared the vegetation, opened the rock at the spring source, and established the bathing facility. == Souvenirs == [[File:Interiors_of_the_%22mara-kannon%22.jpg|thumb|A phallic statue dedicated to Mara Kannon]] * In homage to the legend of the white monkey’s discovery of the hot spring, the "Three Monkeys Manjū"—a baked confection molded in the image of the three wise monkeys ("see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil")—has been a renowned souvenir since its introduction in 1951 (Showa 26). * Approximately 1 km north of Tawarayama Onsen stands a Mara Kannon, where ceramic phallic statues are sold in the town. It is believed that if a wish is written on one and dedicated, the wish will be granted. == Access == * **Railway**: 　– Take {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}} via the {{ill|Mine Line|en|Mine Line|WD=Q898907|lt=Mine Line}} from either {{ill|Nagato-Yumoto Station|en|Nagato-Yumoto Station|WD=Q11654892|lt=Nagato Yumoto Station}} or, alternatively, via the {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=Sanin Main Line}}/Mine Line from {{ill|Nagatoshi Station|en|Nagatoshi Station|WD=Q6958700|lt=Nagato City Station}}. Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "{{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kozuki Station}}," then a limited express to {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}}; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at {{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kōzuki Station}} or {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}} on the {{ill|San'yō Main Line|en|San'yō Main Line|WD=Q1193436|lt=Sanyō Main Line}} and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the {{ill|Chūgoku Expressway|en|Chūgoku Expressway|WD=Q1091207|lt=Chūgoku Expressway}} via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

Board a {{ill|Sanden Transportation|ja|サンデン交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanden Transportation|1=Sanden Kotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11305725}} bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen" or "{{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kozuki Station}}," then a limited express to {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}}; the trip takes 25 minutes from Nagato Yumoto Station and 35 minutes from Nagato City Station. Alternatively, alight at {{ill|Ozuki Station|en|Ozuki Station|WD=Q3611296|lt=Kōzuki Station}} or {{ill|Shimonoseki Station|en|Shimonoseki Station|WD=Q1130253|lt=Shimonoseki Station}} on the {{ill|San'yō Main Line|en|San'yō Main Line|WD=Q1193436|lt=Sanyō Main Line}} and take a Sanden Transportation bus marked "Tawarayama Onsen / Aomi Island" for 70 minutes from Kozuki Station, or 110 minutes from Shimonoseki Station. * **Automobile**: 　– Approximately 30 minutes by car from the {{ill|Chūgoku Expressway|en|Chūgoku Expressway|WD=Q1091207|lt=Chūgoku Expressway}} via the {{ill|Kozuki Interchange|ja|小月インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuki Interchange|1=Ozuki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10959070}}, taking National Route 491 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. Alternatively, about 30 minutes from the {{ill|Mine Interchange|ja|美祢インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine Interchange|1=Mine interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609305}} via National Routes 435 and 316 and the Yamaguchi Prefectural Route 34 Shimonoseki-Nagato Line. == References == == Further Reading == * [[Internal Ministry (Japan)|内務省]] Sanitary Bureau, ed. 『日本鉱泉誌 下』 (Kōkōsha, 1886) * Tamura, Teisuke. 『Tawarayama Onsen-shi』 (1904) * Tawarayama Onsen Guide Committee, ed. 『俵山温泉案内』 (Takahashi Onsendō, 1922) * Tayama, Hanabukuro and Nakazawa, Hiromitsu. 『Onsen Shūyū: The Western Volume』 (Kinseidō, 1922) * {{ill|Nagato Railway|ja|長門鉄道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagato Railway|1=Nagato Railway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654901}} Company Transportation Division, ed. 『Nagato Railway Guide』 (1922) * 『Onsen Guide to Tawarayama』 (Fujii Ryokan, 1936) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju no Yūjiriki―Nihonjin ga Hagukunde Kita Odoro no Kenkōhō』 (Shōden-sha Shinsho, 2005) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Onsen Kyōju: Walking the Ancient Hot Springs』 (Nihon Keizai Shimbun Shuppansha, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Matsuda Kyōju no "Onsen-dō"』 (Chūnishi Shuppan, 2008) * Matsuda, Tadatoshi. 『Why Are Hot Springs Good for the Body?』 (Heibonsha, 2016) == External Links == * [http://tawarayamaonsen.com/ Tawarayama Onsen Gōmei Kaisha] {{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|俵山温泉|version=101558369|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:俵山温泉]] [[zh:温泉]] [[ja:老神温泉]] [[zh:温泉神社]] [[zh:温泉寺]] [[ja:開湯伝説]] {{wikidata link|Q11386420}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Nagato City]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Hot springs of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
